Paul does not command the Corinthians to give. He invites them into something far more searching: the chance to prove what they actually believe. The Macedonians—poor and suffering—have already answered the question. They gave beyond their means, not from obligation but from joy, and they did something Paul emphasizes with startling clarity: "they gave themselves first to the Lord." Their money followed their hearts because their hearts had already been surrendered. Now Paul holds this mirror up to Corinth. You excel in so much, he says. You are gifted and eloquent and knowledgeable. But will you excel in giving? The question cuts deeper than any demand could. He is testing whether your love is real or merely something you speak about.

The foundation for this test is Christ Himself. "Though He was rich, yet for your sake He became poor, so that you through His poverty might become rich." This is not sentimental language about divine sacrifice. It describes the actual movement of God in the Incarnation—the Son of God entering human poverty and limitation, surrendering the independent exercise of divine power, emptying Himself of rights to accomplish your redemption. If you understand what happened at Bethlehem and Calvary, if you truly know this grace, then refusing to share what you have becomes incomprehensible. Not because giving proves you are generous, but because it proves you have actually encountered the One who gave everything. Your giving is the echo of His giving.

Paul is also remarkably practical about this. He does not expect the Corinthians to impoverish themselves while others live comfortably. The goal is equality—not that everyone becomes poor, but that no one hoards while others lack. The surplus of one generation becomes the relief of the next. This reflects God's character throughout Scripture: He does not demand martyrdom from the wealthy but obedience. He asks for open hands, not closed fists. He asks you to remember that what you have is not ultimately yours to keep, and that seasons of abundance are meant to prepare you for seasons of potential need. Meanwhile, Titus and the other brothers are coming not as intimidating authorities but as partners in this work. Their presence itself is a test: will you treat the messengers of the churches with honor? Will your generosity extend to how you welcome them? Your reputation among the churches will be decided not by what you say you believe but by what you do when tested.
